title: API testing basics
short_description: Introduces what an API is, why QA tests APIs separately from the UI, and the building blocks of an API test.
learning_goals:
Explain what an API is and how a client and server communicate through one.
Describe why API testing finds bugs earlier and more reliably than UI testing.
Identify the parts of an API request and response (endpoint, method, headers, body, status code).
Set up a first request in a tool and verify both the data and the status code returned.
theory:
An API (Application Programming Interface) is a contract that lets two pieces of software talk to each other. When you open a weather app, the app (the client) sends a request to a server asking for today's forecast, and the server sends back the data. The UI you see is just one consumer of that data; the API is the actual machinery underneath. API testing means checking that machinery directly, without going through buttons and screens.

QA cares about API testing because it is faster, more stable, and catches problems the UI hides. A UI test can pass even when the backend returns wrong data, because the screen may round, format, or silently swallow it. By calling the API directly you see the raw truth: the exact JSON, the exact status code, the exact headers. API tests also run in milliseconds and don't break when a button moves, so they form the backbone of regression suites.

Every API interaction has a request and a response. A request has a method (GET, POST, etc.), a URL/endpoint (like https://api.example.com/users/42), optional headers (such as Authorization or Content-Type), and an optional body (the data you send, usually JSON). The response has a status code (200 OK, 404 Not Found), headers, and usually a body with the result.

When you test an API you verify three things: the status code is correct, the response body has the right structure and values, and the response time is acceptable. For example, requesting GET /users/42 should return 200 with a JSON object containing id 42; requesting a deleted user should return 404, not a 200 with empty data. Checking both positive and negative cases is core to good API testing.

API testing also lets you reach states that are hard to reproduce in the UI: creating 50 records quickly, sending malformed input, or testing with an expired token. This makes it a powerful tool for boundary and negative testing well before the feature is fully wired into the front end.
key_terms: API, endpoint, request, response, payload, status code, client-server, contract testing
real_world_example: Your team ships a registration feature. The UI shows a generic 'Something went wrong' message when a duplicate email is used. By calling POST /register directly with an already-registered email, you discover the server returns HTTP 409 Conflict with a clear message 'email already in use' that the front end is ignoring. You file a bug against the UI for swallowing a useful backend error, something a UI-only tester would never have seen.
step_by_step:
1. Read the API documentation to find the base URL, the endpoint, the method, and required headers.
2. Open an API client (Postman, Insomnia, or curl).
3. Choose the HTTP method (start with a simple GET).
4. Enter the full endpoint URL, e.g. https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/users/1.
5. Add any required headers, such as Content-Type: application/json or an Authorization token.
6. Send the request and inspect the response.
7. Check the status code is what you expected (e.g. 200).
8. Verify the response body has the right fields and values.
9. Repeat with a negative case (invalid id) and confirm you get an error status like 404.
common_mistakes:
Only checking the status code and ignoring whether the body data is actually correct.
Forgetting required headers (like Authorization) and then blaming the API when you get 401.
Testing only happy paths and never sending invalid or empty input.
Confusing a 200 status with success when the body actually contains an error message.
Hardcoding test data that another test deletes, causing flaky failures.
practical_use_case: QA engineers use API testing every day to smoke-test a build before UI testing, to set up test data quickly (create a user via API instead of clicking through five screens), and to verify integrations between services. It is also the foundation for automated regression suites that run on every deployment.
summary: An API is the contract two programs use to exchange data, and API testing checks that contract directly without the UI. You verify the status code, the response body, and response time, covering both valid and invalid inputs. This finds bugs earlier, runs faster, and is more stable than UI testing.
example:
A first GET request and the checks you'd make, using curl:

```bash
curl -i https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/users/1
```

Expected response (abridged):

```
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Content-Type: application/json; charset=utf-8

{
  "id": 1,
  "name": "Leanne Graham",
  "email": "Sincere@april.biz"
}
```

Checks: status is 200, id equals 1, name and email fields exist and are non-empty. Negative case: `curl -i https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/users/9999` should return 404 with an empty body.
interactive: Using https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/users/3, send a GET request. Write down the status code you got, the value of the 'name' field, and what you'd expect if you requested user id 99999 instead.
expected_answer: The GET request to /users/3 returns status 200 OK. The 'name' field is 'Clementine Bauch'. Requesting /users/99999 returns 404 Not Found with an empty (`{}`) body, because no user with that id exists. A strong answer notes that you check both the status code AND the body, and that the invalid id should NOT return 200.
homework: Pick the public API https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com. Send GET requests to /posts/1, /posts/1/comments, and /posts/99999. For each, record the method, URL, status code, and a one-line description of the body. Identify which request is a negative case and explain why its status code differs.
expected_result: A short table with three rows. /posts/1 returns 200 with a single post object. /posts/1/comments returns 200 with an array of comment objects. /posts/99999 returns 404 with an empty body. The learner correctly labels /posts/99999 as the negative case and explains that the resource does not exist, so the server returns 404 instead of 200.

quiz.question: What does an API primarily allow?
answers: Two pieces of software to communicate and exchange data
Users to style a web page
A database to back itself up automatically
A browser to render images faster

quiz.question: Why do QA engineers test APIs directly instead of only through the UI?
answers: Because the UI is always bug-free
Because API tests are faster, more stable, and reveal the raw response the UI may hide
Because APIs cannot be tested through a UI
Because it avoids writing any test cases

quiz.question: Which of these is NOT part of an HTTP request?
Requesting a user that does not exist should usually return which status?

title: REST API basics
short_description: Explains REST principles, resources, and how predictable URL plus method patterns shape what QA tests.
learning_goals:
Define REST and describe its core principles (resources, statelessness, uniform interface).
Map CRUD operations to HTTP methods on a resource.
Read a REST endpoint URL and predict what it does.
Design basic test cases for a REST resource based on its method and path.
theory:
REST (Representational State Transfer) is an architectural style for designing web APIs. Most modern web services are RESTful. The central idea is the resource: a thing the API exposes, such as a user, an order, or a product. Each resource has a URL (also called a URI), for example /users or /users/42. REST APIs use standard HTTP methods to act on those resources in predictable ways.

The four common operations map cleanly to HTTP methods: GET reads a resource, POST creates one, PUT or PATCH updates one, and DELETE removes one. This is often called CRUD (Create, Read, Update, Delete). Because the pattern is consistent, once you learn it you can predict behavior: GET /products lists products, GET /products/10 reads product 10, POST /products creates a product, DELETE /products/10 deletes it.

REST is stateless, meaning each request carries everything the server needs to handle it (such as an auth token in the header); the server does not remember previous requests. For QA this matters because every request must be self-contained, and you can't rely on a 'logged-in session' persisting on the server side between calls in the way a traditional UI session might.

REST responses use HTTP status codes to communicate outcomes, and bodies are usually JSON. A well-designed REST API returns 201 Created (not just 200) after a successful POST, 204 No Content after a DELETE, and 404 when a resource is missing. As a tester, knowing these conventions lets you spot when an API misbehaves, for example returning 200 after creating a resource instead of 201, or returning 200 for a deletion that didn't actually delete anything.

Resources can be nested to express relationships, like /users/42/orders meaning 'the orders belonging to user 42'. Query parameters refine results without changing the resource, like /products?category=books&limit=10. Understanding this structure lets a tester read an endpoint and immediately design positive, negative, and boundary cases around it.
key_terms: REST, resource, URI, statelessness, CRUD, uniform interface, idempotency, query parameter
real_world_example: You are testing an e-commerce backend. The docs list DELETE /cart/items/{id}. You call it once and get 204 No Content; the item disappears. You call the exact same DELETE again and the API returns 404. You flag this for discussion: DELETE is supposed to be idempotent, so calling it twice on an already-removed item arguably should still succeed quietly (204) rather than error, depending on the team's contract. This sparks a useful conversation about expected REST behavior.
step_by_step:
1. Find the resource you are testing (e.g. 'orders') and its base path /orders.
2. List the operations the API supports for it: GET, POST, PUT/PATCH, DELETE.
3. For each operation, note the expected success status (GET 200, POST 201, DELETE 204).
4. Write a positive case: perform the operation with valid data and confirm the status and body.
5. Write a negative case: use an invalid id or bad data and confirm the correct error status (e.g. 404 or 400).
6. Check resource relationships, like GET /users/1/orders, and verify only that user's orders return.
7. Try query parameters (?status=shipped) and confirm filtering works.
common_mistakes:
Assuming every successful call returns 200, missing that POST should return 201 and DELETE 204.
Confusing PUT (replace the whole resource) with PATCH (update part of it).
Forgetting that REST is stateless and expecting the server to 'remember' a previous request.
Not testing nested resources, so cross-user data leaks (seeing another user's orders) go unnoticed.
Testing only the collection endpoint (/users) and ignoring the single-item endpoint (/users/{id}).
practical_use_case: Every time QA validates a CRUD feature (add to cart, edit profile, cancel order), the work maps to REST operations. Understanding REST lets testers read API docs quickly, generate consistent test cases across resources, and catch convention violations that confuse front-end developers and integrators.
summary: REST organizes an API around resources identified by URLs, acted on with standard HTTP methods that map to CRUD. It is stateless, uses meaningful status codes, and returns JSON. Knowing these conventions lets QA read any REST endpoint and design positive, negative, and boundary tests quickly.
example:
A complete CRUD test plan for a 'todos' resource:

```
Create:  POST   /todos        body {"title":"Buy milk","done":false}  -> expect 201, body has id
Read all:GET    /todos                                              -> expect 200, JSON array
Read one:GET    /todos/{id}                                         -> expect 200, matching id
Update:  PATCH  /todos/{id}    body {"done":true}                    -> expect 200, done=true
Delete:  DELETE /todos/{id}                                         -> expect 204, empty body
Negative:GET    /todos/99999                                        -> expect 404
```

Example create with curl:

```bash
curl -i -X POST https://example.com/todos \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"title":"Buy milk","done":false}'
```

Expected: HTTP/1.1 201 Created, body includes a generated "id".
interactive: Given the endpoint PATCH /users/42 with body {"email":"new@mail.com"}, describe what it does, what success status you expect, and write one negative test case for it.
expected_answer: PATCH /users/42 partially updates user 42, changing only the email field to new@mail.com while leaving other fields untouched. A successful update returns 200 OK with the updated user object (some APIs return 204). A good negative test case: PATCH /users/99999 with the same body should return 404 Not Found because that user does not exist; another valid one is sending an invalid email like {"email":"notanemail"} and expecting 400 Bad Request.
homework: Using https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com, exercise the /posts resource: GET /posts, GET /posts/1, POST /posts (with a JSON body), PATCH /posts/1, and DELETE /posts/1. Record the method, URL, request body (if any), and the status code returned for each. Note which statuses match REST conventions and which don't.
expected_result: A table of five rows showing the method, URL, body, and status. GET calls return 200; POST returns 201; PATCH returns 200; DELETE returns 200. The learner notes that jsonplaceholder is a fake API that returns 200 for DELETE rather than the conventional 204, and explains the difference between the convention and the actual behavior.

quiz.question: In REST, what is a 'resource'?
answers: A thing the API exposes, like a user or an order, addressed by a URL

quiz.question: Which HTTP method is normally used to create a new resource?
What does 'stateless' mean in REST?
answers: The server stores each user's session permanently
Each request contains everything needed; the server doesn't remember prior requests
The API has no status codes

quiz.question: A successful DELETE on a REST resource most conventionally returns which status?

title: Postman basics
short_description: Hands-on introduction to Postman for sending requests, organizing them in collections, and adding test assertions.
learning_goals:
Send GET and POST requests in Postman and read the response panel.
Use environment variables to avoid hardcoding URLs and tokens.
Organize requests into a collection and run them in sequence.
Write simple test assertions in the Tests tab to validate status and body.
theory:
Postman is the most widely used GUI tool for API testing. Instead of typing curl commands, you build requests visually: pick the method from a dropdown, type the URL, fill in headers and body using tabs, and click Send. The response appears in a panel below, showing status code, response time, size, and a formatted body (Pretty view for JSON). This makes it ideal for QA who are learning APIs.

The core unit of organization is the Collection: a saved group of requests, often mirroring a feature or an API. Inside collections you can create folders, and each request is saved so you and your team can re-run it. Collections can be exported and shared, which is how teams hand over API test suites.

Environments and variables keep tests maintainable. Instead of hardcoding https://staging.example.com in 30 requests, you define a variable {{base_url}} and a variable {{token}} in an environment (e.g. 'Staging' vs 'Production'). Switching environments retargets every request at once. You reference a variable with double curly braces, like {{base_url}}/users/1.

Postman's real power for QA is the Tests tab, where you write JavaScript assertions that run after the response arrives. For example pm.test("status is 200", () => pm.response.to.have.status(200)) automatically passes or fails. You can assert on the body too, like checking pm.response.json().id === 1. These turn a manual click-and-look into a repeatable automated check.

The Collection Runner executes every request in a collection in order, with its tests, and shows a pass/fail summary. Combined with data files (CSV/JSON), you can run the same request with many inputs (data-driven testing). This bridges manual API testing toward automation without writing a full test framework.
key_terms: collection, request, environment variable, pre-request script, Tests tab, assertion, Collection Runner, pm object
real_world_example: Your team's API moved from staging to a new staging URL. Because every request used {{base_url}} from an environment, you changed one variable and all 40 saved requests instantly pointed to the new host, no editing needed. Then you ran the whole collection in the Collection Runner before the demo and immediately saw that 3 of 40 requests returned 500, catching a broken endpoint minutes before the stakeholder review.
step_by_step:
1. Install Postman and create a new Collection named after your feature.
2. Add a request: choose GET, enter https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/users/1, click Send.
3. Inspect the response: confirm 200 OK and view the Pretty JSON body.
4. Create an Environment with a variable base_url = https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com.
5. Change the request URL to {{base_url}}/users/1 and select the environment from the top-right dropdown.
6. Open the Tests tab and add: pm.test("status 200", () => pm.response.to.have.status(200));
7. Send again and check the Test Results tab shows 1 passing.
8. Save the request to the collection, then open the Collection Runner and run all requests.
common_mistakes:
Hardcoding URLs and tokens in every request instead of using environment variables.
Forgetting to select an environment, so {{base_url}} resolves to nothing and the request fails.
Writing requests but never adding assertions, so a wrong body still 'looks green'.
Putting the body in the wrong format (selecting form-data when the API expects raw JSON).
Not saving requests, then losing all work when Postman closes.
practical_use_case: QA uses Postman daily to explore new endpoints, reproduce backend bugs, build shareable regression collections, set up test data, and run smoke tests before UI testing. Postman collections are often committed to the repo and run in CI via Newman, the command-line runner.
summary: Postman lets you build, send, and organize API requests visually, then add JavaScript assertions to validate them automatically. Collections group requests, environment variables keep URLs and tokens DRY, and the Collection Runner executes everything with a pass/fail report. It is the standard tool for manual and semi-automated API testing.
interactive: Create a Postman request to GET {{base_url}}/posts/1 where base_url = https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com. Write two assertions in the Tests tab: one for the status code and one verifying the response body contains a non-empty 'title' field.
homework: Build a Postman collection called 'JSONPlaceholder Smoke' with four saved requests: GET /users, GET /users/1, GET /posts/1, and POST /posts. Use an environment variable for base_url. Add at least one status-code assertion to each request. Run the whole collection in the Collection Runner and export the collection as JSON.
expected_result: An exported collection JSON file containing four requests, each referencing {{base_url}}, each with at least a status-code test. A Collection Runner summary screenshot showing all requests executed with their pass/fail results (GET requests passing 200, POST passing 201). The base_url variable is defined in an environment, not hardcoded.

quiz.question: What is a Postman Collection?
answers: A saved, shareable group of requests

quiz.question: How do you reference an environment variable named base_url in a Postman URL?
Where do you write JavaScript assertions that run after a response is received?
Which Postman feature runs every request in a collection and reports pass/fail?

title: HTTP methods
short_description: Covers the main HTTP methods, what each does to a resource, and the safety and idempotency properties QA must test.
learning_goals:
Describe the purpose of GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, and DELETE.
Explain the difference between PUT and PATCH and between safe and idempotent methods.
Predict the correct method for a given user action.
Design tests that verify a method has the right side effects (or none).
theory:
HTTP methods (also called verbs) tell the server what action to perform on a resource. The five you'll use most are GET (retrieve data), POST (create a new resource or trigger an action), PUT (replace a resource entirely), PATCH (update part of a resource), and DELETE (remove a resource). Choosing the right method is part of correct API design, and verifying it is part of QA.

Two properties matter a lot for testing. A method is safe if it doesn't change server state: GET, HEAD, and OPTIONS are safe, meaning calling them should never create, modify, or delete data. A method is idempotent if calling it multiple times has the same effect as calling it once: GET, PUT, and DELETE are idempotent, while POST is not. So sending POST /orders twice usually creates two orders, but sending PUT /users/42 twice leaves user 42 in the same final state.

The PUT vs PATCH distinction trips up many testers. PUT replaces the whole resource, so any field you omit may be wiped or reset to default. PATCH updates only the fields you send and leaves the rest alone. If you PUT /users/42 with only {"email":"x@y.com"}, a strict server might erase the user's name and phone. Testing this requires checking the full resource after the call, not just the field you changed.

Methods carry semantic expectations QA should verify. A GET must never have side effects, so a bug where GET /deleteUser?id=5 actually deletes a user is a serious design and security flaw. DELETE should remove the resource and typically return 204. POST that creates should return 201 with the new resource's id or location.

Knowing methods also helps with negative testing: sending the wrong method to an endpoint should return 405 Method Not Allowed. For example POST to a read-only /health endpoint should be rejected, not silently accepted. These method-level checks catch security and design issues that body-only testing misses.
key_terms: GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE, safe method, idempotency, 405 Method Not Allowed
real_world_example: While testing a profile-edit feature, you notice the front end uses PUT /users/42 but only sends the changed field. After the save, the user's phone number disappears. By calling the API directly you confirm the server treats PUT as a full replace and nulls out omitted fields. You file a bug: either the front end should send PATCH, or it should send the complete user object with PUT. This is a real data-loss bug only method knowledge revealed.
step_by_step:
1. Identify the user action (view, create, edit, delete) and the resource it affects.
2. Map the action to the expected method (view=GET, create=POST, full edit=PUT, partial edit=PATCH, remove=DELETE).
3. Send the request and verify the expected success status (200/201/204).
4. For GET, confirm no data changed on the server (re-fetch and compare).
5. For POST, send it twice and confirm two resources are created (non-idempotent).
6. For PUT/DELETE, send it twice and confirm the final state is identical (idempotent).
7. For PUT, fetch the full resource afterward and check that omitted fields were not unintentionally wiped.
8. Send a disallowed method to the endpoint and confirm you get 405.
common_mistakes:
Treating PUT and PATCH as interchangeable and missing data-loss bugs from PUT replacing whole resources.
Assuming GET is harmless without verifying it truly causes no side effects.
Not testing idempotency, so duplicate POSTs creating duplicate records slip through.
Expecting 200 for a creation when 201 (or 204 for deletion) is correct.
Forgetting to test that wrong methods return 405 rather than being silently accepted.
practical_use_case: Every CRUD test, every form submission, and every API security review involves choosing and verifying methods. QA uses method knowledge to catch duplicate-submission bugs, accidental data loss on edits, unsafe GETs, and improperly exposed endpoints.
summary: HTTP methods tell the server what to do: GET reads, POST creates, PUT replaces, PATCH partially updates, DELETE removes. Safe methods cause no change and idempotent methods give the same result when repeated. Verifying the right method, its side effects, and its status code catches data-loss, duplicate-record, and security bugs.
example:
Testing idempotency and the PUT-vs-PATCH difference:

```bash
# POST is NOT idempotent: two calls create two orders
curl -X POST https://api.example.com/orders -d '{"item":"book"}'  # -> 201, id 101
curl -X POST https://api.example.com/orders -d '{"item":"book"}'  # -> 201, id 102 (duplicate!)

# PATCH updates only the email, name is preserved
curl -X PATCH https://api.example.com/users/42 -d '{"email":"new@x.com"}'
# GET shows: {"id":42,"name":"Ann","email":"new@x.com"}  name intact

# PUT replaces the whole resource; omitting name may wipe it
curl -X PUT https://api.example.com/users/42 -d '{"email":"new@x.com"}'
# GET shows: {"id":42,"name":null,"email":"new@x.com"}  name lost!
```
interactive: A user clicks 'Cancel order' once but accidentally double-clicks, firing the request twice. Which HTTP method should the cancel endpoint use to be safe against double-clicks, and why? What status should each of the two calls return?
expected_answer: The cancel action should use an idempotent method. If cancellation is modeled as deleting the order, DELETE /orders/{id} is appropriate; if it sets a status, PUT /orders/{id}/status with a fixed value works. Both DELETE and PUT are idempotent, so the second (duplicate) call has the same final effect as the first. The first call returns 204 (DELETE) or 200 (PUT). The second call should leave the order in the same cancelled state; depending on the contract it returns 204/200 again or 404/409 if already cancelled, but it must NOT cancel twice or cause an error that confuses the user. POST should be avoided here because it is not idempotent.
homework: For a simple blogging API with a /posts resource, write a one-page method test plan. For each of GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE: state the action, the expected success status, whether it is safe and/or idempotent, and one test verifying that property. Include a test that sends a disallowed method and expects 405.
expected_result: A table covering all five methods. GET marked safe and idempotent (200), POST not idempotent (201), PUT idempotent and not safe (200), PATCH not necessarily idempotent (200), DELETE idempotent (204). Each row has a concrete verification, e.g. 'POST twice creates two posts', 'PUT twice yields identical resource'. The plan includes a 405 test such as 'PATCH /health -> 405'.

quiz.question: Which method is used to partially update a resource, changing only the fields you send?
Which property means calling a method multiple times has the same effect as calling it once?
Which method is NOT idempotent and may create duplicates if sent twice?
Sending a method an endpoint doesn't allow should return which status?

title: HTTP status codes
short_description: Explains the five HTTP status code classes and the specific codes QA checks most, with what each signals.
learning_goals:
Name the five status code classes (1xx-5xx) and what each represents.
Recall the meaning of common codes: 200, 201, 204, 301, 400, 401, 403, 404, 409, 422, 429, 500.
Distinguish client errors (4xx) from server errors (5xx) and route bugs accordingly.
Use status codes as a primary assertion in API tests.
theory:
HTTP status codes are three-digit numbers the server returns to summarize what happened with a request. They are grouped into five classes by their first digit: 1xx informational, 2xx success, 3xx redirection, 4xx client error (the request was wrong), and 5xx server error (the server failed). For QA, the class alone is a fast diagnostic: a 4xx means 'we (the caller) did something wrong', while a 5xx means 'the server is broken'.

The key success codes are 200 OK (generic success, used for GET and many others), 201 Created (a POST successfully created a resource), and 204 No Content (success with nothing to return, typical after DELETE). Returning the wrong success code (200 instead of 201 after a create) is a minor but real contract bug worth noting.

The 4xx family is where most interesting QA work lives. 400 Bad Request means malformed input. 401 Unauthorized means you're not authenticated (no or bad token). 403 Forbidden means you're authenticated but not allowed. 404 Not Found means the resource doesn't exist. 409 Conflict signals a clash like a duplicate email. 422 Unprocessable Entity means the syntax was fine but the data failed validation. 429 Too Many Requests means rate limiting kicked in. The 401-vs-403 distinction matters: confusing them leaks information or blocks legitimate users.

The 5xx family signals server-side failure: 500 Internal Server Error (an unhandled exception), 502 Bad Gateway, and 503 Service Unavailable. A 5xx is almost always a bug to file against the backend, because a well-behaved API should turn bad input into a 4xx, never crash into a 500. So a 500 when you send invalid data is itself a defect: the validation should have caught it and returned 400/422.

For QA, status codes are the first and most reliable assertion. They are unambiguous, language-independent, and stable. A strong test asserts the exact expected code for each case: 200 for a valid read, 404 for a missing resource, 401 without a token, 403 with the wrong role, and never a 500 for foreseeable bad input.
key_terms: 2xx success, 4xx client error, 5xx server error, 200 OK, 201 Created, 401 Unauthorized, 403 Forbidden, 404 Not Found
real_world_example: You submit a registration form with an email that's already taken. The API returns 500 Internal Server Error. You recognize this as a backend bug: a duplicate email is foreseeable user input and should produce a 409 Conflict (or 422) with a clear message, never a server crash. You file a high-priority bug noting that the 500 also exposes a stack trace, which is a security concern. The fix is to add validation that returns 409.
step_by_step:
1. For each test case, decide the expected status code before sending the request.
2. Send the request and read the status code first, before looking at the body.
3. If the class is 2xx, verify it's the right success code (201 for create, 204 for delete).
4. If you sent bad input and got 4xx, confirm it's the specific correct code (400/401/403/404/409/422).
5. If you got a 5xx for normal bad input, file a bug: the server should have returned a 4xx.
6. Test auth boundaries: no token -> expect 401; valid token, wrong role -> expect 403.
7. Add the status assertion as the first check in your automated test.
common_mistakes:
Treating any 2xx as 'pass' without checking it's the correct code (201 vs 200).
Confusing 401 (not authenticated) with 403 (authenticated but forbidden).
Accepting a 500 for invalid input instead of recognizing it as a defect.
Checking only the body and ignoring the status code entirely.
Assuming 404 is always a bug, when it's the correct response for a genuinely missing resource.
practical_use_case: Status codes are the backbone of every API assertion, smoke test, and bug triage. QA uses them to quickly classify failures (client vs server), validate auth and permission rules, confirm CRUD outcomes, and catch crashes that should have been graceful errors.
summary: HTTP status codes summarize each request's outcome in five classes: 2xx success, 3xx redirect, 4xx client error, 5xx server error. QA asserts the exact expected code for every case and treats 5xx on foreseeable bad input as a defect, since the API should return a 4xx instead. The 200/201/204 and 401/403/404 distinctions are especially important.
example:
A status-code test matrix for a login + profile feature:

```
Scenario                                  Expected status
----------------------------------------  ---------------
Valid login                               200 OK
Wrong password                            401 Unauthorized
Valid token, GET own profile              200 OK
Valid token, GET another user's profile   403 Forbidden
GET /users/99999 (no such user)           404 Not Found
Register with existing email              409 Conflict
Register with missing required field      400 Bad Request
Register with badly typed field           422 Unprocessable Entity
Send invalid JSON that crashes server     500  <-- BUG, should be 400
```

The last row is the kind of defect QA exists to catch: invalid input must never produce a 500.
interactive: For each situation, give the most appropriate status code: (a) a user requests a product page that was deleted, (b) a user with a valid login tries to access an admin-only page, (c) a request arrives with no authentication token, (d) the server throws an unhandled exception.
expected_answer: (a) 404 Not Found, because the product resource no longer exists. (b) 403 Forbidden, because the user is authenticated but lacks permission for the admin page. (c) 401 Unauthorized, because no valid credentials were provided. (d) 500 Internal Server Error, the generic server-side failure code, which QA should file as a backend bug. A strong answer also notes that 401 is about authentication while 403 is about authorization.
homework: Take any public API (e.g. https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com or https://httpstat.us) and deliberately trigger at least six different status codes: a 200, a 201, a 400 or 404, a 401 or 403 (if available), and a 500 (httpstat.us/500 returns one on demand). For each, record the request you sent and the code received, and write one sentence on what the code means.
expected_result: A table of at least six rows: request, status code, and a one-line meaning. The learner correctly distinguishes 4xx client errors from 5xx server errors, identifies which were success (2xx) vs error responses, and explains, for example, that 404 means the resource was not found while 500 means the server itself failed. Using httpstat.us to force specific codes is acceptable and encouraged.

quiz.question: Which status class indicates the SERVER failed to handle a valid request?
A POST that successfully creates a new resource should ideally return which code?
What is the difference between 401 and 403?
answers: 401 means not authenticated; 403 means authenticated but not allowed
401 is a server error; 403 is a success

quiz.question: You send clearly invalid input and the API returns 500. What should QA conclude?
answers: It's a defect; the server should return a 4xx like 400 instead of crashing
It means the resource was created

title: JSON basics
short_description: Teaches JSON syntax, data types, and structure so QA can read, validate, and assert on API payloads.
learning_goals:
Read JSON and identify objects, arrays, and the six value types.
Write valid JSON by hand for a request body.
Navigate to a nested value using dot and bracket paths.
Spot common JSON syntax errors that break a request.
theory:
JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) is the most common format for sending data in API requests and responses. It's plain text that's easy for humans to read and for machines to parse. As a QA engineer you'll read JSON in nearly every API response and write it in nearly every POST or PATCH body, so fluency here is essential.

JSON has two container types. An object is wrapped in curly braces {} and holds key-value pairs, like {"name": "Ann", "age": 30}. Keys must be strings in double quotes. An array is wrapped in square brackets [] and holds an ordered list of values, like [1, 2, 3] or a list of objects. Objects and arrays nest freely, which is how real-world data like 'a user with a list of orders' is represented.

JSON values come in six types: string ("hello", always double-quoted), number (42 or 3.14, no quotes), boolean (true or false), null (an explicit empty value), object, and array. Common mistakes come from type confusion: "42" is a string but 42 is a number, and a test that expects a number will fail if the API returns a quoted string. true is a boolean, but "true" is a string.

To assert on data you navigate paths. Given {"user": {"address": {"city": "Kyiv"}}}, the city is at user.address.city. For arrays you use an index: orders[0].id is the id of the first order. Most API tools and test frameworks use this dot/bracket notation, so being able to write the path to any value is a core skill.

Validity is strict. JSON requires double quotes (not single), forbids trailing commas, requires commas between items, and doesn't allow comments. A single misplaced comma or a missing quote makes the whole payload invalid and the server typically responds 400 Bad Request. QA frequently uses a JSON validator or the Pretty view in Postman to catch these before blaming the API.
key_terms: object, array, key-value pair, string, number, boolean, null, nesting
real_world_example: A POST /orders keeps returning 400. The developer insists the endpoint works. You open the request body in a JSON validator and find a trailing comma after the last field: {"item":"book","qty":2,}. JSON forbids trailing commas, so the payload is invalid and never reaches the business logic. You remove the comma, the request succeeds with 201, and you note in the bug tracker that the issue was malformed test data, not the API.
step_by_step:
1. Identify whether the top-level structure is an object {} or an array [].
2. For an object, list the keys and note each value's type (string, number, boolean, null, object, array).
3. For nested data, write the dot path to the value you care about (e.g. data.user.email).
4. For arrays, use an index to reach an element (e.g. items[0].price).
5. When writing a body, wrap all keys and string values in double quotes.
6. Leave numbers, booleans, and null unquoted.
7. Separate items with commas but never put a comma after the last one.
8. Paste the result into a JSON validator to confirm it's valid before sending.
common_mistakes:
Using single quotes instead of double quotes around keys or strings.
Leaving a trailing comma after the last item in an object or array.
Quoting numbers or booleans ("42" or "true") so type assertions fail.
Forgetting commas between key-value pairs, breaking the whole payload.
Confusing an object {} with an array [] when writing the path to a value.
practical_use_case: QA reads JSON in every API response to verify field values, writes JSON for POST/PATCH bodies to set up test data, and writes JSON paths in assertions (Postman, REST Assured, schema validators). Understanding JSON types is also key to boundary testing of numeric and string fields.
summary: JSON is the text format for API data, built from objects {}, arrays [], and six value types. Keys and strings use double quotes; numbers, booleans, and null are unquoted; trailing commas and single quotes are invalid. QA reads JSON to verify responses, writes it for request bodies, and uses dot/bracket paths to assert on nested values.
interactive: Given the JSON {"product":{"name":"Laptop","price":999,"inStock":false,"tags":["sale","new"]}}, write the path to the price, the path to the second tag, and state the data type of inStock.
expected_answer: Path to price: product.price (value 999, a number). Path to the second tag: product.tags[1] (value "new", a string; arrays are zero-indexed so index 1 is the second element). Data type of inStock: boolean (value false, unquoted). A strong answer notes that 999 is a number not a string, and that tags is an array of strings.
homework: Write, by hand, a valid JSON object representing a customer with: an id (number), a fullName (string), an isVip flag (boolean), a nullable nickname, an address object containing street and city, and an orders array with two order objects each having orderId and amount. Validate it in any JSON validator and fix any errors. Then write the dot/bracket path to the second order's amount.
expected_result: A syntactically valid JSON object (passes a validator) containing all required fields with correct types: id and orderId/amount as numbers, fullName/street/city as quoted strings, isVip as an unquoted boolean, nickname as null, address as a nested object, and orders as an array of two objects. The path to the second order's amount is correctly given as orders[1].amount.

quiz.question: Which is valid JSON?
What data type is the value in {"active": true}?
Given {"items":[{"id":1},{"id":2}]}, what is the path to the second item's id?
Why might a server reject a JSON body with 400 Bad Request?
answers: It had a trailing comma or single quotes, making it invalid

title: SQL basics for QA
short_description: Introduces relational databases and SQL so QA can verify data directly instead of trusting the UI.
learning_goals:
Explain what a relational database, table, row, and column are.
Describe why QA queries the database to verify backend state.
Run a basic SELECT to read data from a table.
Understand primary keys and foreign keys and how they relate tables.
theory:
Most applications store their data in a relational database such as PostgreSQL, MySQL, or SQL Server. Data lives in tables, which are like spreadsheets: each table has columns (fields, like email or created_at) and rows (records, one per entity such as one user). SQL (Structured Query Language) is the language you use to read and manipulate that data. For QA, SQL is how you check what actually got saved, independent of what the screen shows.

QA uses the database for verification. When you register a user through the UI, you can query the users table to confirm the row exists, the email is stored correctly, the password is hashed (not plain text), and the status is 'active'. The UI might show a success message even if the save partially failed; the database tells the truth. SQL also lets you set up and clean up test data far faster than clicking through screens.

Tables relate to each other through keys. A primary key is a column (often id) that uniquely identifies each row; no two rows share the same primary key. A foreign key is a column in one table that points to the primary key of another, modeling relationships. For example an orders table has a user_id foreign key pointing to users.id, expressing 'this order belongs to that user'. Understanding keys lets you trace data across tables and spot orphaned or mismatched records.

The most important statement for QA is SELECT, which reads data without changing it. A basic query is SELECT column1, column2 FROM table_name; or SELECT * FROM users; to get every column. Because SELECT is read-only it's safe to run on most environments. QA should be extremely cautious with UPDATE, DELETE, and INSERT, which modify data and can corrupt a shared test environment if run carelessly (always use a WHERE clause and a transaction when changing data).

Reading SQL is a transferable skill that pays off across testing, debugging, and reporting. Even non-automation manual testers benefit enormously: a single query can confirm in seconds what would take many UI steps, and it pinpoints whether a bug is in the front end, the API, or the data layer.
key_terms: relational database, table, row, column, primary key, foreign key, SELECT, query
real_world_example: A tester registers a new user and the UI shows 'Welcome'. To be sure, they run SELECT id, email, password_hash, status FROM users WHERE email = 'test@mail.com';. The row exists and status is 'active', but the password_hash column contains the plain-text password instead of a hash. This is a critical security defect the UI completely hid. The tester files a high-severity bug, backed by the exact query and result as evidence.
step_by_step:
1. Get read-only access and connection details for the test database (host, port, user, password, db name).
2. Connect using a client like DBeaver, pgAdmin, or a command-line tool.
3. List the tables to learn the schema (in many clients, expand the database tree).
4. Identify the table relevant to your feature (e.g. users, orders).
5. Run SELECT * FROM users LIMIT 10; to preview columns and sample data.
6. Narrow to the columns you care about: SELECT id, email, status FROM users;.
7. After an action in the app, re-query to confirm the data changed as expected.
8. Never run UPDATE/DELETE on shared data without a WHERE clause and confirmation.
common_mistakes:
Running UPDATE or DELETE without a WHERE clause and wiping or altering the whole table.
Assuming the UI's success message means the data was saved correctly.
Forgetting that SELECT * returns every column, which is slow and noisy on wide tables.
Confusing primary keys and foreign keys when tracing relationships.
Querying production instead of the test database.
practical_use_case: QA queries the database daily to verify saved data, set up and clean up test records, reproduce data-specific bugs, confirm hashing/encryption, and check that background jobs updated the right rows. It is a core skill for both manual and automation testers.
summary: Relational databases store data in tables of rows and columns, linked by primary and foreign keys, and SQL reads and changes that data. QA uses SELECT to verify what the backend actually saved, independent of the UI. Read operations are safe; modifying statements must always use WHERE and care to avoid corrupting shared environments.
example:
Verifying a registration and a relationship across two tables:

```sql
-- Confirm the user was saved with a hashed password and active status
SELECT id, email, password_hash, status
FROM users
WHERE email = 'test@mail.com';

-- Trace the orders that belong to that user (foreign key user_id -> users.id)
SELECT o.id, o.total, o.created_at
FROM orders o
WHERE o.user_id = 42;
```

Expected: the first query returns one row with a long hash (not the plain password) and status 'active'; the second returns the orders linked to user 42 via the foreign key.
interactive: You just created a product named 'Wireless Mouse' through the admin UI. Write a SELECT query to confirm it exists in a products table and check its price and stock columns.
expected_answer: A correct query is: SELECT id, name, price, stock FROM products WHERE name = 'Wireless Mouse'; This reads only the relevant columns and filters by the product name. A strong answer explains that you verify the row exists, the price matches what was entered, and stock has the expected value, and that SELECT is read-only so it's safe to run. Selecting just the needed columns (rather than SELECT *) is preferred.
homework: Using any sample database (e.g. the Sakila or Chinook sample DBs, or a local app DB), do the following and record the query plus a sentence of explanation for each: (1) list the columns of one table, (2) select the first 10 rows of that table, (3) select only 2-3 specific columns, (4) identify the primary key of the table and one foreign key pointing to another table.
expected_result: Four queries with explanations. (1) A way to inspect the schema/columns. (2) SELECT * FROM <table> LIMIT 10;. (3) SELECT col1, col2 FROM <table>;. (4) A correct identification of the primary key (e.g. id) and a foreign key (e.g. customer_id referencing customers.id), with a sentence explaining the relationship. The learner demonstrates they only ran read-only queries.

quiz.question: In a relational database, what is a 'row'?
What does a foreign key do?
answers: Uniquely identifies each row in its own table
Points to the primary key of another table to model a relationship
Deletes related rows automatically by default

quiz.question: Why does QA query the database directly?
answers: To verify what was actually saved, independent of the UI

quiz.question: Which statement is read-only and safe to run for verification?

title: SELECT queries
short_description: Goes deeper into SELECT, covering columns, aliases, sorting, limiting, and counting rows for verification.
learning_goals:
Write SELECT statements that return specific columns rather than everything.
Sort results with ORDER BY and limit them with LIMIT.
Count rows and use simple aggregate functions like COUNT.
Use aliases and DISTINCT to make results clearer and verify uniqueness.
theory:
SELECT is the workhorse of QA database verification. Its basic shape is SELECT <columns> FROM <table>;. Choosing explicit columns (SELECT id, email FROM users) instead of SELECT * makes results readable and queries faster, and it documents exactly what you're checking. You can rename a column in the output with an alias using AS, like SELECT total AS order_total FROM orders, which is handy when columns have cryptic names.

ORDER BY controls the order of results, which matters when you want the newest or largest record. SELECT id, created_at FROM users ORDER BY created_at DESC returns the most recently created users first; ASC (the default) sorts ascending. This is how you find 'the latest order' or 'the highest-priced product' to verify a feature that should surface exactly that record.

LIMIT caps how many rows come back, which keeps verification fast on large tables. Combined with ORDER BY, LIMIT is powerful: SELECT * FROM orders ORDER BY total DESC LIMIT 1 gives you the single most expensive order. Some databases use TOP (SQL Server) or FETCH FIRST instead of LIMIT, but the idea is the same.

Aggregate functions summarize data. COUNT(*) returns the number of rows, which QA uses constantly: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM users WHERE status = 'active' verifies how many active users exist after a migration or bulk action. Other aggregates include SUM, AVG, MIN, and MAX, useful for checking totals and ranges. DISTINCT removes duplicates, so SELECT DISTINCT country FROM users lists each country once, helping you verify a dropdown's possible values or spot unexpected data.

For verification work, a precise SELECT is often the fastest path to a confident yes/no. Want to confirm a bulk import added exactly 500 records? COUNT them. Want to confirm sorting in the UI matches the database? ORDER BY the same column and compare. These small, exact queries are the bread and butter of data-driven QA.
key_terms: SELECT, column list, alias (AS), ORDER BY, ASC/DESC, LIMIT, COUNT, DISTINCT
real_world_example: After running a 'deactivate inactive users' batch job, the product owner expects exactly 1,200 users to be deactivated. You verify with SELECT COUNT(*) FROM users WHERE status = 'inactive'; and get 1,187. The job under-deactivated by 13. You then run SELECT id FROM users WHERE status = 'active' AND last_login < '2024-01-01' to find the 13 that were missed, giving developers the exact ids to investigate. The COUNT and the targeted SELECT turned a vague 'something's off' into a precise bug report.
step_by_step:
1. Decide exactly what you need to verify and which columns prove it.
2. Write SELECT with an explicit column list, e.g. SELECT id, email, status FROM users;.
3. Add ORDER BY to surface the record you care about (e.g. ORDER BY created_at DESC).
4. Add LIMIT to keep the result small (e.g. LIMIT 10 or LIMIT 1 for the top record).
5. To count, use SELECT COUNT(*) FROM <table>; optionally with a condition.
6. Use DISTINCT to verify the set of unique values in a column.
7. Use AS to rename columns for a clearer result.
8. Compare the result against the expected value from the requirement.
common_mistakes:
Using SELECT * on huge tables when only a couple of columns are needed.
Forgetting ORDER BY and then assuming the rows come back in a reliable order (they don't).
Misreading COUNT(*) vs COUNT(column), which can differ when a column has NULLs.
Applying LIMIT without ORDER BY and expecting the 'first' or 'newest' row.
Forgetting that DISTINCT applies to the whole selected row, not just one column, when multiple columns are selected.
practical_use_case: QA uses SELECT with ORDER BY, LIMIT, and COUNT to verify batch jobs, confirm record counts after imports/migrations, find the newest or top record a feature should show, check UI sort order against the database, and audit the set of allowed values in a column.
summary: SELECT retrieves data; choosing explicit columns, sorting with ORDER BY, capping with LIMIT, counting with COUNT, and de-duplicating with DISTINCT turn it into a precise verification tool. ORDER BY is required for any 'newest' or 'top' query, and COUNT is the fastest way to confirm how many rows match a condition.
example:
Common verification queries:

```sql
-- Just the columns you need
SELECT id, email, status FROM users;

-- The 5 most recently registered users
SELECT id, email, created_at
FROM users
ORDER BY created_at DESC
LIMIT 5;

-- The single most expensive order, with a clearer column name
SELECT id, total AS order_total
FROM orders
ORDER BY total DESC
LIMIT 1;

-- How many active users exist
SELECT COUNT(*) AS active_users
FROM users
WHERE status = 'active';

-- The distinct set of countries present
SELECT DISTINCT country FROM users;
```
interactive: Write a single SELECT query that returns the 3 highest-priced products from a products table, showing only the name and price columns, with the price column aliased as 'price_usd'.
expected_answer: A correct query: SELECT name, price AS price_usd FROM products ORDER BY price DESC LIMIT 3; This selects only the two needed columns, aliases price to price_usd, sorts descending so the highest prices come first, and limits to 3 rows. A strong answer notes that ORDER BY price DESC is essential (without it, LIMIT 3 returns an arbitrary three rows) and that DESC means highest first.
homework: On a sample database, write and run five queries, recording each query and its result: (1) select three specific columns from a table, (2) the 10 newest rows by a date column, (3) a COUNT of all rows in the table, (4) a COUNT of rows matching a condition, (5) a DISTINCT list of values in one column. Briefly explain what each result tells you.
expected_result: Five working queries with results and explanations. (1) explicit column list. (2) ORDER BY <date> DESC LIMIT 10. (3) SELECT COUNT(*) FROM <table>. (4) SELECT COUNT(*) FROM <table> WHERE <condition>. (5) SELECT DISTINCT <column> FROM <table>. Explanations show the learner understands ORDER BY is needed for 'newest', that COUNT returns a number, and what the distinct set reveals.

quiz.question: Which clause sorts query results from newest to oldest by created_at?
What does SELECT COUNT(*) FROM users return?
answers: The list of all user emails
The number of rows in the users table

quiz.question: To reliably get the single most expensive order, you should use:
What does DISTINCT do?
answers: Removes duplicate values from the result
Limits the rows to a number

title: Filtering data
short_description: Covers the WHERE clause and operators to filter rows precisely for targeted data verification.
learning_goals:
Filter rows with WHERE using comparison operators.
Combine conditions with AND, OR, and NOT and control precedence with parentheses.
Use LIKE, IN, BETWEEN, and IS NULL for pattern, set, range, and null checks.
Write precise queries that isolate exactly the records a test cares about.
theory:
The WHERE clause filters which rows a query returns, and it's where SELECT becomes a precise QA instrument. Instead of pulling a whole table and scanning by eye, you ask the database an exact question: SELECT * FROM users WHERE status = 'active' returns only active users. Comparison operators include = (equals), <> or != (not equals), <, >, <=, and >=. For example WHERE age >= 18 finds adults, and WHERE total > 100 finds large orders.

Conditions combine with AND, OR, and NOT. AND requires all conditions to be true; OR requires at least one. Parentheses control precedence, which matters a lot: WHERE country = 'UA' AND (status = 'active' OR status = 'trial') is very different from omitting the parentheses. Beginners often get wrong results because AND binds tighter than OR, so always parenthesize mixed conditions.

Several operators handle common patterns. LIKE does pattern matching with wildcards: % matches any sequence of characters and _ matches one character, so WHERE email LIKE '%@gmail.com' finds all Gmail users. IN checks membership in a set: WHERE status IN ('active','trial') is cleaner than chained ORs. BETWEEN checks an inclusive range: WHERE created_at BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-01-31' finds January records, including both endpoints.

NULL needs special handling. NULL means 'unknown/absent' and you cannot compare it with =. WHERE phone = NULL returns nothing; you must write WHERE phone IS NULL or WHERE phone IS NOT NULL. This is a frequent QA trap, because a query that should find users with no phone number silently returns zero rows if you used = NULL.

Precise filtering is the heart of data verification. Want to confirm no user is under 18 after an age-gate feature? SELECT COUNT(*) FROM users WHERE age < 18 should be 0. Want to find the exact records a bug affects? Filter to them. Mastering WHERE turns vague checks into exact, evidence-backed assertions.
key_terms: WHERE, comparison operators, AND/OR/NOT, LIKE, wildcard, IN, BETWEEN, IS NULL
real_world_example: A feature is supposed to block registration for anyone under 18. After release, you verify with SELECT id, age FROM users WHERE age < 18 AND created_at >= '2024-06-01';. The query returns 4 rows, meaning four underage users registered after the age gate went live. You attach the query and the four ids to a bug report. The developers find the validation only ran on the client and could be bypassed via the API, exactly the kind of gap a precise WHERE clause exposes.
step_by_step:
1. State the exact condition that defines the rows you care about.
2. Start with SELECT <columns> FROM <table> WHERE <condition>;.
3. Use the right comparison operator (=, <>, <, >, <=, >=) for the data type.
4. Combine multiple conditions with AND/OR and wrap mixed ones in parentheses.
5. For text patterns use LIKE with % and _ wildcards.
6. For 'one of several values' use IN (...); for ranges use BETWEEN a AND b.
7. For missing values use IS NULL or IS NOT NULL, never = NULL.
8. Run it and compare the row count/values against what the requirement expects.
common_mistakes:
Using = NULL instead of IS NULL, which silently returns zero rows.
Mixing AND and OR without parentheses and getting unintended results.
Forgetting that LIKE patterns need % wildcards (LIKE 'gmail' won't match emails containing gmail).
Assuming BETWEEN is exclusive, when it includes both endpoints.
String vs number quoting errors, like WHERE age = '18' on a numeric column or forgetting quotes around text values.
practical_use_case: QA uses WHERE constantly to isolate the exact rows a test case concerns: verifying validation rules (no underage users, no negative balances), finding records affected by a bug, confirming a filter in the UI matches the database, checking date ranges for reports, and locating rows with missing required data.
summary: The WHERE clause filters rows so you can ask the database an exact question. Comparison operators, AND/OR/NOT with parentheses, and LIKE/IN/BETWEEN/IS NULL cover most filtering needs. The biggest traps are using = NULL instead of IS NULL and mixing AND/OR without parentheses; getting these right makes verification precise and reliable.
example:
A set of precise filtering queries:

```sql
-- Active or trial users in Ukraine (note the parentheses!)
SELECT id, email, status
FROM users
WHERE country = 'UA'
  AND (status = 'active' OR status = 'trial');

-- All Gmail addresses
SELECT email FROM users WHERE email LIKE '%@gmail.com';

-- Orders placed in January 2024 (inclusive range)
SELECT id, total, created_at
FROM orders
WHERE created_at BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-01-31';

-- Users with no phone number on file
SELECT id, email FROM users WHERE phone IS NULL;

-- Verify no underage users exist (should return 0)
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM users WHERE age < 18;
```
interactive: Write a query that finds all orders with a total between 50 and 200 (inclusive) that are NOT cancelled, returning the order id and total. Assume there is a status column where cancelled orders have status = 'cancelled'.
expected_answer: A correct query: SELECT id, total FROM orders WHERE total BETWEEN 50 AND 200 AND status <> 'cancelled'; BETWEEN 50 AND 200 covers the inclusive range, and status <> 'cancelled' (or status != 'cancelled') excludes cancelled orders. A strong answer notes BETWEEN includes both 50 and 200, and that <> means 'not equal'. If cancelled status could be NULL, an even more careful answer adds OR status IS NULL, but excluding by <> is acceptable for the stated assumption.
homework: On a sample database, write six filtering queries and record each query, the row count returned, and a one-line purpose: (1) an equality filter, (2) a numeric range with BETWEEN, (3) a pattern match with LIKE, (4) a set membership with IN, (5) a combined AND/OR condition using parentheses, (6) a query finding rows where some column IS NULL. Explain why = NULL would not have worked for #6.
expected_result: Six correct queries with row counts and purposes. The combined condition in #5 uses parentheses to control precedence. Query #6 uses IS NULL, and the explanation correctly states that NULL cannot be compared with =, so = NULL always returns no rows. The learner demonstrates LIKE with a % wildcard and BETWEEN as inclusive.

quiz.question: How do you correctly find rows where the phone column has no value?
Which query finds all emails ending in @company.com?
WHERE created_at BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-01-31' includes which dates?
answers: Only dates strictly between, excluding both ends
Both endpoints and everything in between (inclusive)

quiz.question: Why do you wrap mixed AND/OR conditions in parentheses?
answers: Because AND binds tighter than OR, so without parentheses the logic may be wrong
Because parentheses are required after every WHERE
To make the query run faster

title: JOIN basics
short_description: Explains how JOINs combine rows from related tables so QA can verify data that spans multiple tables.
learning_goals:
Explain why data is split across related tables and joined back together.
Write an INNER JOIN connecting two tables on a key.
Distinguish INNER JOIN from LEFT JOIN and predict which rows each returns.
Use JOINs to detect orphaned or mismatched data across tables.
theory:
Relational databases split data across tables to avoid duplication. A users table holds user details and an orders table holds orders, with orders referencing users through a user_id foreign key. To answer 'which user placed this order?' you need data from both tables at once, and that's what a JOIN does: it combines rows from two (or more) tables based on a related column.

The most common is the INNER JOIN, which returns only rows that have a match in both tables. The syntax is SELECT ... FROM orders o INNER JOIN users u ON o.user_id = u.id. The ON clause states how the tables relate (here, the order's user_id equals the user's id). Table aliases (o and u) keep the query short and let you qualify columns like u.email and o.total. INNER JOIN is what you use for normal 'show me orders with their user info' queries.

The other essential type is LEFT JOIN (LEFT OUTER JOIN), which returns every row from the left table and the matching rows from the right table, filling in NULL where there's no match. This is invaluable for finding gaps. SELECT u.id FROM users u LEFT JOIN orders o ON u.id = o.user_id WHERE o.id IS NULL finds users who have never placed an order, because their right-side columns come back NULL. INNER JOIN would silently drop those users.

For QA, JOINs verify cross-table integrity. After importing orders, you can confirm every order points to a real user (an orphaned order with a user_id that matches no user is a data bug). You can verify totals, check that a deleted user's orders were handled correctly, and confirm the UI's combined views (like an order history page) match the underlying joined data.

Getting the JOIN condition right is critical. Forgetting the ON clause (or writing a wrong condition) produces a Cartesian product, multiplying every row of one table by every row of the other, which floods you with nonsense rows. Always join on the correct key, and choose INNER vs LEFT deliberately based on whether you want only-matches or all-left-rows-including-gaps.
key_terms: JOIN, INNER JOIN, LEFT JOIN, ON clause, foreign key, table alias, orphaned record, Cartesian product
real_world_example: A reporting bug says some orders show 'Unknown customer' in the admin panel. You investigate with a LEFT JOIN: SELECT o.id, o.user_id FROM orders o LEFT JOIN users u ON o.user_id = u.id WHERE u.id IS NULL;. It returns 7 orphaned orders whose user_id points to users that were hard-deleted. The JOIN pinpoints the exact orders and proves the cause: deleting users didn't reassign or soft-delete their orders. You hand developers the 7 ids and the root cause in one report.
step_by_step:
1. Identify the two tables that hold the data you need and the key that links them (the foreign key).
2. Decide the JOIN type: INNER for only-matching rows, LEFT to keep all rows from the main table.
3. Write FROM <leftTable> <alias> JOIN <rightTable> <alias> ON <leftAlias>.<key> = <rightAlias>.<key>.
4. Select qualified columns (e.g. u.email, o.total) so it's clear which table each comes from.
5. Run it and check the row count is sensible (not exploded into a Cartesian product).
6. To find gaps, use LEFT JOIN and filter WHERE <rightAlias>.<key> IS NULL.
7. Compare the joined result against the UI's combined view to verify they match.
common_mistakes:
Forgetting the ON clause and producing a huge Cartesian product of every row combination.
Using INNER JOIN when you needed LEFT JOIN, silently dropping rows with no match (and missing orphans).
Joining on the wrong columns (e.g. id = id instead of order.user_id = user.id).
Not using table aliases, then getting 'ambiguous column' errors when both tables share a column name like id.
Forgetting that LEFT JOIN's unmatched right-side columns are NULL, so filtering them needs IS NULL.
practical_use_case: QA uses JOINs to verify any feature whose data spans tables: order histories, user-role mappings, product-category listings, and report pages. LEFT JOINs are the standard way to find data-integrity problems like orphaned records, users with no profile, or orders with no line items.
summary: JOINs combine rows from related tables on a key so you can verify data that spans more than one table. INNER JOIN returns only matching rows; LEFT JOIN returns all rows from the left table with NULLs where the right has no match, which is how you find orphaned or missing data. Always join on the correct key and use the right type, or you'll get dropped rows or a Cartesian explosion.
example:
Joining orders to users, and finding orphaned orders:

```sql
-- Normal join: each order with its customer's email
SELECT o.id AS order_id, o.total, u.email
FROM orders o
INNER JOIN users u ON o.user_id = u.id
ORDER BY o.created_at DESC;

-- Find users who have never placed an order
SELECT u.id, u.email
FROM users u
LEFT JOIN orders o ON u.id = o.user_id
WHERE o.id IS NULL;

-- Find orphaned orders whose user no longer exists (data-integrity bug)
SELECT o.id, o.user_id
FROM orders o
LEFT JOIN users u ON o.user_id = u.id
WHERE u.id IS NULL;
```
interactive: You need a list of every product together with its category name. Products are in a products table with a category_id; categories are in a categories table with id and name. Write the JOIN query, and say which JOIN type you'd use if some products have no category and you still want them listed.
expected_answer: Basic query: SELECT p.name AS product, c.name AS category FROM products p INNER JOIN categories c ON p.category_id = c.id; If some products have no category (category_id is NULL or points nowhere) and you still want those products listed, use a LEFT JOIN: SELECT p.name AS product, c.name AS category FROM products p LEFT JOIN categories c ON p.category_id = c.id; The uncategorized products appear with category as NULL. A strong answer explains that INNER JOIN would drop products with no matching category, while LEFT JOIN keeps them.
homework: On a sample database with at least two related tables, write four queries with results and explanations: (1) an INNER JOIN combining the two tables on their key, (2) the same data but with a LEFT JOIN, and explain how the row counts differ, (3) a LEFT JOIN with a WHERE ... IS NULL to find rows in the left table with no match, (4) one query that intentionally omits a correct ON condition or otherwise demonstrates why the result is wrong, with an explanation of the Cartesian product.
expected_result: Four queries with results. The INNER JOIN returns only matched rows; the LEFT JOIN returns at least as many rows, including unmatched left rows with NULLs. Query (3) correctly uses IS NULL on a right-table column to find unmatched left rows. Query (4) shows or explains a Cartesian product (rows multiplied) and why a proper ON clause prevents it. The learner uses table aliases and qualified column names.

quiz.question: What does an INNER JOIN return?
answers: All rows from both tables regardless of matches
Only rows that have a match in both tables
Only rows from the left table

quiz.question: Which JOIN keeps every row from the left table, filling NULLs where the right has no match?
To find users who have never placed an order, you would:
answers: LEFT JOIN users to orders and filter WHERE orders.id IS NULL

quiz.question: Forgetting the ON clause in a JOIN typically causes:
answers: A Cartesian product, multiplying every row of one table by every row of the other
Only the first row to return

title: Git basics for QA
short_description: Introduces Git fundamentals QA needs to read history, switch branches, and collaborate on test code and docs.
learning_goals:
Explain what Git and a repository are and why version control matters for QA.
Run the core workflow: clone, branch, add, commit, push, pull.
Read a commit history and a diff to understand what changed.
Resolve a simple merge conflict and avoid common Git mistakes.
theory:
Git is a distributed version control system that tracks changes to files over time. A repository (repo) is a project folder Git is watching, including its full history. Even manual QA engineers benefit: test cases, automation scripts, API collections, and documentation increasingly live in Git, and reading history helps you understand exactly what changed in a build you're testing. Knowing the commit that introduced a bug is often the fastest path to a clear report.

The core workflow revolves around a few commands. git clone <url> copies a remote repo to your machine. You work on a branch, an independent line of changes, created with git checkout -b my-branch (or git switch -c). You stage changes with git add <file>, record them with git commit -m "message", and share them with git push. To get others' changes you run git pull. Branching matters because it isolates work: your test updates don't disturb the main code until they're reviewed and merged.

Reading history is a daily QA skill. git log shows commits (who changed what, when, and why), git diff shows the exact line-by-line changes, and git blame shows who last touched each line. When a regression appears, scanning recent commits on the relevant files often reveals the likely cause, which you can cite in the bug report. Tools like GitHub/GitLab present the same information visually through commit lists and pull request diffs.

Collaboration happens through pull requests (PRs) or merge requests. A developer opens a PR with their changes; QA can review the diff, leave comments, and check that tests were added. After approval and a green CI run, the branch merges into main. Understanding this flow helps QA know what's actually in a build and trace a feature to the code that implements it.

Merge conflicts happen when two people change the same lines. Git marks the conflict in the file with <<<<<<<, =======, and >>>>>>> markers; you edit the file to keep the correct version, remove the markers, then add and commit. The most important Git discipline for QA is to commit small, write clear messages, pull before you start, and never force-push to shared branches.
key_terms: repository, clone, branch, commit, push/pull, merge, pull request, merge conflict
real_world_example: A login bug appears in today's build that wasn't there yesterday. You open the auth files in GitHub and run git log on them, spotting a commit from this morning titled 'refactor token validation'. You view its diff and see the expiry check was changed from >= to >. You attach the commit hash and the one-line diff to your bug report. Developers confirm the off-by-one almost immediately, turning a vague 'login broke' into a precise, fast fix.
step_by_step:
1. Clone the repo: git clone <url> and cd into it.
2. Pull the latest main: git checkout main then git pull.
3. Create a branch for your work: git checkout -b qa/update-login-tests.
4. Make your changes (e.g. edit a test case file or Postman collection).
5. See what changed: git status and git diff.
6. Stage and commit: git add . then git commit -m "Add boundary tests for login".
7. Push the branch: git push -u origin qa/update-login-tests.
8. Open a pull request, request review, and after approval merge it.
9. If you hit a conflict on pull/merge, edit the marked file, remove the conflict markers, then add and commit.
common_mistakes:
Committing directly to main instead of working on a branch.
Writing vague commit messages like 'fix' or 'update' that say nothing about what changed.
Forgetting to pull before starting, then hitting avoidable merge conflicts.
Using git push --force on a shared branch and overwriting teammates' work.
Leaving conflict markers (<<<<<<<, =======, >>>>>>>) in the file after a botched merge.
practical_use_case: QA uses Git to store and version test cases and automation, read history to pinpoint when a bug was introduced, review pull request diffs to know what a build contains, branch their own changes safely, and collaborate with developers through PRs and CI.
summary: Git tracks file changes over time in repositories, with branches isolating work until it's reviewed and merged via pull requests. The core loop is clone, branch, add, commit, push, pull. For QA, reading log/diff/blame to find what changed and when is as valuable as making commits, and disciplined small commits with clear messages keep collaboration smooth.
example:
A complete QA branch workflow:

```bash
# Start from the latest main
git checkout main
git pull

# Create a branch for your test changes
git checkout -b qa/login-boundary-tests

# After editing test files, review and commit
git status
git diff
git add tests/login_test_cases.md
git commit -m "Add boundary tests for password length (0, 1, 8, 64, 65)"

# Share it and open a PR
git push -u origin qa/login-boundary-tests

# Later, find when a bug was introduced in a file
git log --oneline auth/token.py
git diff a1b2c3d~1 a1b2c3d   # view the suspect commit's changes
```
interactive: You cloned the repo, edited a test-cases file, and want to share it for review without touching the main branch. List the exact Git commands in order, from creating a branch to having the changes ready for a pull request.
expected_answer: A correct sequence: 1) git checkout -b qa/my-test-changes (create and switch to a new branch). 2) git add <file> (stage the edited test-cases file). 3) git commit -m "<clear message describing the change>" (record it). 4) git push -u origin qa/my-test-changes (push the branch to the remote). 5) Open a pull request on GitHub/GitLab and request review. A strong answer notes pulling latest main first (git pull on main) and writing a descriptive commit message, and emphasizes that working on a branch keeps main untouched until review.
homework: Create a free GitHub repository (or use a provided sandbox). Clone it, create a branch named qa/practice, add a file test-cases.md containing two sample test cases, commit with a clear message, and push the branch. Open a pull request to main. Then, using git log and git diff on the repo, write a short note describing one commit's hash, message, and what it changed.
expected_result: Evidence of a branch named qa/practice with at least one commit containing test-cases.md and a descriptive commit message (not just 'update'). An opened pull request targeting main. A short written note correctly reading the history: a commit hash, its message, and a plain-English summary of the diff (what lines were added/changed). No commits made directly to main.

quiz.question: What is a Git branch?
answers: An independent line of changes isolated from the main code until merged

quiz.question: Which command records your staged changes with a message?
To find which recent change likely introduced a bug in a file, you would use:
answers: git log and git diff on that file

quiz.question: What should you do when you encounter a merge conflict?
answers: Force-push to overwrite the other branch
Edit the file to keep the correct content, remove the conflict markers, then add and commit
Ignore it; Git resolves conflicts automatically

title: Jira basics
short_description: Teaches how to use Jira issues, workflows, and boards to track bugs and tasks the way real QA teams do.
learning_goals:
Identify the common Jira issue types and when to use each.
Create a clear, well-structured bug ticket with the right fields.
Move issues through a workflow and understand statuses and transitions.
Use JQL and boards to find and organize the issues you own.
theory:
Jira is the most widely used issue-tracking tool in software teams. Everything in Jira is an 'issue', a single tracked work item. Common issue types include Epic (a large body of work), Story (a user-facing feature), Task (a piece of work), Sub-task (a breakdown of a parent), and Bug (a defect). As QA, you'll mostly create Bugs and sometimes Tasks, and you'll link your bugs to the Story they were found against so everyone sees the connection.

A good bug ticket is the QA's primary deliverable, and Jira's fields structure it. Key fields are Summary (a concise title), Description (steps to reproduce, expected vs actual result, environment), Priority/Severity (how urgent/impactful), Affects Version (which build), Attachments (screenshots, logs, videos), and Assignee. A vague ticket like 'login broken' wastes everyone's time; a strong one lets a developer reproduce the issue in minutes without asking you a single follow-up question.

Issues move through a workflow, a defined sequence of statuses connected by transitions. A typical bug flow is To Do -> In Progress -> In Review -> In QA / Ready for Test -> Done, often with a Reopened path when a fix fails verification. QA frequently owns the verification step: when a developer marks a bug fixed, the ticket comes to you to retest, and you either transition it to Done or reopen it with notes on why it still fails.
